concept: proclitic preposition bāʾ contributes the relation
prompt: In بِسْمِ, what does the bāʾ proclitic contribute and what case does its noun take?
expected_answer: The bāʾ is a prefixed preposition carrying the in/with sense; اسم after it is in jarr (kasra). Read it as بـ + a jarr noun, so the phrase is in the name of, not a host-only name.
accepted_variants: bāʾ = in/with, host noun اسم in jarr; in the name of
prefixed preposition بـ plus a genitive noun
forbidden_answers: name only
the bāʾ is decoration
host-only gloss is enough
required_reasoning: jarr case named for the noun after بـ

concept: definite article is part of the noun, not a standalone gloss
prompt: In ٱللَّهِ with the article ٱلْ, why does ٱلْ not produce its own hover word?
expected_answer: The ٱلْ is the definite article the, but it is part of the noun and only contributes definiteness; it never produces a standalone gloss and must not be duplicated as a second the.
accepted_variants: article ال marks definiteness once, folds into the noun
ال is the, but not its own hover word
forbidden_answers: the + the
the article is its own word
ال is a separate particle to gloss

concept: proclitic preposition lām and its jarr noun
prompt: In لِلَّهِ, what does the lām proclitic contribute and what does it do to its noun?
expected_answer: The lām = to / for / belonging to; in لِلَّهِ it is to/for Allah and the lām puts its noun in jarr.
accepted_variants: lām = to/for/belonging to; noun in jarr
for, to (belonging to); لـ governs jarr
forbidden_answers: the lām is decoration
host-only gloss is enough
Allah only
required_reasoning: relation not copied from external wording

concept: iḍāfa supplies an implied of with no written particle
prompt: In رَبِّ ٱلْعَالَمِينَ, is there a preposition meaning of, and where does the of come from?
expected_answer: There is no written preposition; رَبِّ ٱلْعَالَمِينَ is an iḍāfa, a noun-to-noun construct, and the of is implied by the construct, not a written word. The learner must not invent a particle.
accepted_variants: it is an iḍāfa; of is implied, no written particle
noun-to-noun construct, no preposition between them
forbidden_answers: there is a hidden preposition of in the text
invent a particle for of
of is a written word here
required_reasoning: implied of attributed to the construct

concept: proclitic wāw default conjunction; strip before reading next word
prompt: In وَإِيَّاكَ, what does the wāw proclitic contribute by default, and what must you do before reading the next word?
expected_answer: The wāw default sense is the conjunction and; strip the wāw before reading the next word. The oath and circumstantial senses need a specific frame and are not the default.
accepted_variants: wāw = and (default conjunction); strip it first
and; oath/circumstantial only with a specific frame
forbidden_answers: wāw is always an oath
the wāw is a radical
read the next word without stripping the wāw
required_reasoning: proclitic wāw stripped before reading next word

concept: attached pronoun ـنا is an object piece, not a separate word
prompt: In ٱهْدِنَا, what is the suffix ـنا and is it a separate word on the line?
expected_answer: The suffix ـنا = us, an attached object pronoun on the verb ٱهْدِ (guide). It is one of the most frequent function pieces and is not a separate word on the line.
accepted_variants: ـنا = us, attached object pronoun, not a standalone word
object clitic ـنا on the verb
forbidden_answers: ـنا is a separate standalone word
ـنا is part of the root
the suffix can be dropped from the hover
required_reasoning: clitic distinguished from a standalone word

concept: relative pronoun heads a clause and agrees with its referent
prompt: What is ٱلَّذِينَ in صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ, and what does it agree with?
expected_answer: ٱلَّذِينَ is the relative pronoun those who (masculine plural); it heads a relative clause modifying the noun before it and agrees in number and gender with its referent.
accepted_variants: relative pronoun those who; agrees with its referent in number/gender
masc. plural relative heading a relative clause
forbidden_answers: it is a demonstrative this
agreement does not matter
it is the subject noun itself

concept: multi-function لَا decided by the frame, here coordinating exclusion
prompt: After غَيْرِ, what does لَا contribute in لَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ, and why is a single fixed gloss unsafe?
expected_answer: Here لَا coordinates a second excluded group, giving and not / nor (the astray). لَا is multi-function (plain negation, there-is-no, prohibition); the frame decides, so a single fixed gloss is unsafe.
accepted_variants: nor; لَا coordinates the second excluded group here
frame decides لَا; here coordinating exclusion = nor
forbidden_answers: لَا always means no
لَا always means there is no
one fixed gloss covers لَا everywhere
required_reasoning: frame named as deciding the sense
coordinating-exclusion reading justified by the غَيْرِ frame

concept: unambiguous preposition فِي plus an attached pronoun
prompt: Decompose فِيهِ and name the case of the attached pronoun.
expected_answer: فِيهِ = فِي (in) + clitic ـهِ (it), giving in it. فِي is unambiguous in spelling (fāʾ + yāʾ) and its noun or pronoun is in jarr.
accepted_variants: فِي in + ـه it = in it; pronoun in jarr
preposition فِي plus object pronoun ـه
forbidden_answers: فِيهِ is one indivisible word with no pronoun
the pronoun is not in the token
فِي needs frame disambiguation like بـ
required_reasoning: jarr case named for the pronoun

concept: lām preposition before a definite noun with elided article alif
prompt: In لِلْمُتَّقِينَ, segment the lām from its noun and explain the لِلْ spelling.
expected_answer: لِلْمُتَّقِينَ = لِـ (for) + ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (the God-conscious) = for the God-conscious. The article alif elides after the lām (لِلْ), so read the lām as the preposition and the rest as its noun.
accepted_variants: لِـ for + the God-conscious; article alif elides after lām
preposition lām plus a definite noun, alif elided
forbidden_answers: لِلْ is a single particle
the second lām is the article alif intact
host-only gloss is enough
required_reasoning: lām preposition segmented from the article+noun

concept: bāʾ that carries a verb's transitivity fuses with the verb
prompt: In ٱلَّذِينَ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ, what does the bāʾ in بِٱلْغَيْبِ do, and how does its gloss behave?
expected_answer: The bāʾ here carries the verb's transitivity (آمَنَ بـ = believe in), so its gloss fuses with the verb rather than reading as a free preposition; ٱلَّذِينَ is the relative those who and ٱلْغَيْبِ is the unseen.
accepted_variants: bāʾ carries آمَنَ بـ transitivity; its gloss fuses with the verb
relative those who + believe in the unseen, bāʾ bound to the verb
forbidden_answers: the bāʾ is a free in with no verb link
host-only the unseen is enough
the bāʾ is decoration
required_reasoning: gloss fused with the verb not read free

concept: strip a proclitic stack; guard مِنْ vs مَنْ by the harakah
prompt: Decompose وَمِمَّا and name the homograph guard on its content particle.
expected_answer: وَمِمَّا = وَ (and) + مِنْ (from / of) + مَا (that which), giving and from that which. The content particle is مِنْ (kasra mīm = from), not مَنْ (who); read the harakah to keep them apart.
accepted_variants: and from that which; مِنْ from (kasra) not مَنْ who
wāw + مِنْ + relative مَا; guard the mīm harakah
forbidden_answers: مَنْ who is the content particle
read the stack without stripping the wāw
مِنْ and مَنْ are interchangeable
required_reasoning: proclitic stack stripped piece by piece
مِنْ vs مَنْ decided by the harakah

concept: detached pronoun هُمْ vs clitic ـهُمْ
prompt: In هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ, is هُمْ detached or attached, and how does it differ from ـهُمْ?
expected_answer: هُمْ here is the detached pronoun they, a standalone word heading the clause. It must be distinguished from the clitic ـهُمْ (them) attached to a noun, verb, or preposition.
accepted_variants: detached pronoun they; differs from attached ـهُمْ them
standalone هُمْ vs suffix ـهُمْ
forbidden_answers: هُمْ is always attached
detached and clitic pronouns are the same
هُمْ is part of the next verb
required_reasoning: subject role of detached pronoun named

concept: emphasis particle إِنَّ read by hamza seat plus shadda
prompt: In إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ, what marks إِنَّ and how do you keep it apart from أَنَّ and أَنْ?
expected_answer: إِنَّ is hamza-on-alif with a kasra seat plus shadda on the nūn = indeed / verily. Read it by seat plus shadda to keep it apart from أَنَّ (fatḥa seat = that) and أَنْ (fatḥa, no shadda = to/that).
accepted_variants: indeed/verily; kasra seat + shadda distinguishes it from أَنَّ/أَنْ
read seat+shadda, not first glance
forbidden_answers: إِنَّ and أَنَّ are the same word
read it by the first letter only
shadda is decoration

concept: preposition عَلَىٰ with alif maqṣūra; certify the root
prompt: In عَلَىٰ هُدًى, gloss عَلَىٰ and name the root guard.
expected_answer: عَلَىٰ (written with alif maqṣūra ى) = on / upon; here upon guidance, with its noun in jarr. Its root is ʿ-l-w/y, not whatever norm() might suggest after dropping the seat; the clitic عَلَيْهِ shifts the yāʾ out.
accepted_variants: upon; root ʿ-l-w/y, noun in jarr
عَلَىٰ = on/upon, certify the root not the norm key
forbidden_answers: upon is host-only with no jarr
trust the norm key for the root
عَلَىٰ has no weak letter
required_reasoning: root certified against the norm key

concept: preposition إِلَىٰ; the hamza seat is load-bearing against a false root
prompt: In إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا, gloss إِلَىٰ and explain why the hamza seat must be certified.
expected_answer: إِلَىٰ = to / toward (goal/terminus). The hamza seat is load-bearing: إِلَىٰ and its clitic إِلَيْهِ are root ʾ-l-y, not root ل-ي-ن; norm() drops the seat and creates that false match, so the seat must be certified.
accepted_variants: to/toward; root ʾ-l-y, certify the hamza seat
goal preposition; guard against the false ل-ي-ن match
forbidden_answers: root is ل-ي-ن
the hamza seat can be dropped
norm key is enough for the root

concept: qamustyping4 function-token cluster must not be phrase-only
prompt: In أَمْ لَهُمْ, why are أَمْ and لَهُمْ separate grammar-bearing pieces for a rich hover?
expected_answer: أَمْ is a function particle in the clause, while لَهُمْ is lām plus the attached pronoun هُمْ. The English phrase may read smoothly, but the teaching layer must preserve the particle, the relation, and the pronoun; otherwise the cluster is phrase-only and not rich-hover complete.
accepted_variants: أم is a particle; لهم = ل + هم, relation plus pronoun
preserve the particle and the lām-pronoun pieces, not one phrase gloss
forbidden_answers: one phrase hover is enough
ignore the pronoun
لهم is just a host word
أم can stay uncolored
required_reasoning: أم classified as a function particle
